executive_summary:
This Community Scorecard exercise engaged 1,000 community members across six provinces of Zambia — Copperbelt, Southern, Lusaka, Eastern, Central, and Northern — with full completion, as part of a participatory social accountability process jointly assessing health and education service delivery, consistent with CHS practice and SDG 3, SDG 4, and SDG 16.

Community sentiment is favourable (54.8%). Service availability is the leading scorecard theme (6 mentions), followed by action plan follow-through (5), staff attitude (4), community participation (3), and accountability (3). Action plan follow-through appearing as a leading, near-top theme is a particularly important scorecard-specific finding: communities are explicitly evaluating whether previous scorecard commitments were kept, not only current service conditions.

Provincial coverage spans both more urban Copperbelt and Lusaka and more rural Northern and Central provinces, with reasonably even participation (186 to 130), supporting a credible basis for both facility-level and provincial-level accountability dialogue.
key_findings: Service availability is the leading theme (6 mentions) — whether staff, medicine, and learning materials are actually present and accessible remains communities' top concern across both health and education services.

Action plan follow-through (5 mentions, a close second) is a distinctly scorecard-specific finding — communities are evaluating whether commitments from previous scorecard sessions were kept, a direct test of the accountability mechanism's own credibility.
Staff attitude (4 mentions) remains a meaningful concern across both sectors, distinct from service availability itself.
Community participation and accountability are tied as a secondary tier (3 mentions each) — communities want both to be involved in planning and to see institutional accountability for outcomes.

The near-top ranking of action plan follow-through suggests that if this scorecard cycle's own commitments are not visibly acted upon, it could specifically undermine confidence in the NEXT scorecard round, given communities are already tracking this dimension explicitly.
Provincial coverage spanning urban and rural contexts (Copperbelt/Lusaka to Northern/Central) supports using this data for both facility-level dialogue and provincial social accountability planning.
discussion: Action plan follow-through ranking as the second-highest theme, just behind service availability itself, is the single most important structural finding for the scorecard programme's own credibility: it means communities are not simply reporting on current conditions, they are actively tracking whether the accountability mechanism itself delivers on its promises. If this cycle's action items are not visibly implemented before the next scorecard round, it is reasonable to expect this theme's prominence to grow further, potentially undermining confidence in the entire participatory process. This makes visible, documented follow-through on this cycle's specific commitments not just good practice, but a direct determinant of the scorecard programme's own continued legitimacy.
conclusions: This scorecard cycle confirms service availability as the top substantive concern across health and education services jointly, while identifying action plan follow-through as a near-equally prominent, scorecard-specific concern that speaks directly to the credibility of the accountability process itself. Visible, documented implementation of this cycle's action items is essential before the next round.

immediate: Present service availability and action-plan-follow-through findings jointly at the next facility-community dialogue, with an explicit accounting of which previous action items were completed, partially completed, or not started.
Assign a specific, named responsible party (community or facility) for each of this cycle's action items, given the demonstrated community attention to follow-through specifically.
medium_term: Establish a simple, visible tracking mechanism (e.g. a posted scorecard action log at the facility) so communities can see follow-through progress between formal scorecard sessions, not only at the next full cycle.

Investigate staff attitude concerns jointly across health and education facilities to determine whether this reflects a shared underlying issue (e.g. staffing levels, training) or facility-specific circumstances.
long_term: Institutionalize action-plan-follow-through as its own standing, explicitly tracked scorecard indicator, given evidence this is now a near-top community concern in its own right.
Use this cycle's provincial distribution as the baseline sampling framework for future rounds to maintain comparability across both urban and rural service contexts.

risks: If this cycle's action items are not visibly completed before the next scorecard round, action-plan-follow-through concern is likely to grow further, risking the credibility of the participatory accountability process itself.

Treating service availability and staff attitude as generic complaints, rather than jointly investigating whether they share a root cause across health and education facilities, risks a less effective response.
opportunities: A simple, low-cost visible action-log at each facility could directly address the action-plan-follow-through concern without requiring a major new accountability structure.

Because this scorecard jointly covers health and education, a shared root-cause investigation (e.g. of staff attitude or service availability) could yield efficiencies not available to single-sector scorecard exercises.
lessons_learned: This cycle confirms that community scorecard participants are evaluating the accountability mechanism's own track record, not only current service conditions — a structural insight that should inform how every future scorecard session is opened (with an explicit review of prior action-item status) rather than starting fresh each cycle as if it were the first round.
